DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-13-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: DEFENDANT CENTRAL COAST AGRICULTURE, INC.'s DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant's "amended demurrers" are off calendar. They are untimely and procedurally improper. Plaintiff filed the first amended complaint ("FAC") on 6/5/23. On 8/9/23, the court heard defendant's demurrers to that pleading and issued an order on 8/10/23. The court sustained without leave to amend as to cause of action 1 and overruled as to causes of action 2-4. Per CRC 3.1320(g) and (j), defendant should have filed an answer to the FAC within 10 days of notice of entry of that order. There was nothing for plaintiff to amend and plaintiff never filed a second amended complaint. But instead of complying with the code and rules, defendant filed these "amended demurrers" and a motion to strike the FAC. There is no basis for defendant to be filing serial or piecemeal demurrers to the FAC. In fact, CCP 430.41(b) provides that a party should not be raising new arguments to an amended complaint that could have been raised to a prior version of the complaint. Defendant's conduct is even more violative of this principle because plaintiff never amended the FAC. Defendant to answer by December 20, 2023.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/RBU)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-24-2023 Continued To Nov-01-23 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-09-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT CENTRAL COAST AGRICULTURE, INC.'S DEMURRERS TO PLAINTIFFS' CLASS ACTION FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

No appearances. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Central Coast Agriculture, Inc.'s demurrer is sustained in part without leave to amend and overruled in part.

The limited role of a demurrer is to test the legal sufficiency of a complaint. (Donabedian v. Mercury Ins. Co. (2004) 116 Cal.App.4th 968, 994.) The demurrer is treated as admitting all material facts properly pleaded, but not contentions, deductions or conclusions of fact or law. (Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.)

1. California Trademark Infringement and Dilution

Plaintiffs (Galactic Brands IP, LLC and Gold Drop Holdings, Inc.) brought claims against Defendant for infringement and dilution under California trademark law. The first amended complaint (FAC) alleges that Plaintiffs are the assignees of the LIQUID DIAMONDS and GOLD DROP marks from Accentian, Inc., and Forward Focus Management, LLC, respectively. (FAC 27, 38.)

Defendants argue that the FAC must allege that Plaintiffs' assignments were made in writing. Not so. "To survive a demurrer, the complaint need only allege facts sufficient to state a cause of action; each evidentiary fact that might eventually form part of the plaintiff's proof need not be alleged." (C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, 872.) Plaintiffs' allegations in the FAC are sufficient. (FAC 27, 38.)

Further, the request for judicial notice of 'publicly available records' is denied. "The hearing on demurrer may not be turned into a contested evidentiary hearing through the guise of having the court take judicial notice" (Del E. Webb Corp. v. Structural Materials Co. (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d 593, 605.)

Therefore, the demurrer is overruled as to the second and third causes of action.

2. Lanham Act

Plaintiffs concede that they cannot assert federal trademark rights on these marks. (Pl.'s Opp. at 7:11.) Instead, Plaintiffs argue that they can assert federal claims on lawful state trademark registrations based on lawful intrastate use of cannabis products.

But this argument fails for the same reason. "[O]nly lawful use in commerce can give rise to trademark priority. [T]o hold otherwise would be to put the government in the 'anomalous position' of extending the benefits of trademark protection to a seller based upon actions the seller took in violation of that government's own laws." (CreAgri, Inc. v. USANA Health Sciences, Inc. (2007) 474 F.3d 626, 630.) Because cannabis and marijuana are Schedule 1 substances under the federal Controlled Substances Act, Plaintiffs cannot assert these federal claims.

"[P]ermitting plaintiff to amend the complaint would serve no useful purpose, given the fact that the actions of petitioner, as set forth in plaintiff's original complaint, cannot give rise to a cause of action." (Mercury Cas. Co. v. Sup. Ct. (2005) 134 Cal.App.4th 1027, 1035.) The demurrer is therefore sustained as to the first cause of action without leave to amend.

3. Unfair Competition

Defendants argue that dismissal of the other causes of action necessitates dismissal of the unfair competition claim. But because the court overruled the demurrer on the California trademark infringement and dilution claims, the demurrer is also overruled as to the fourth cause of action for unfair competition.

Order signed. Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (302/RBU)
